summary: Controls the official machine policy for Cross-Device Experiences. The current app writes EnableCdp = 0 as part of the broader bundle, but this child record tracks only the documented machine policy while the user-side CDP preset values remain in the deprecated parent audit trail.

setting.name: Cross-Device Experiences Machine Policy
setting.casual_explanation: Windows can let devices belonging to the same user discover each other and continue some experiences across devices. This policy controls the official machine-level part of that feature.
setting.why_people_change_it: People turn it off for privacy or to avoid device discoverability, while others keep it on for cross-device convenience.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Turning it off reduces discoverability and continue experiences. Unlike the broader parent bundle, this record is just the documented machine policy and is fully sourced.
setting.professional_notes: GroupPolicy.admx documents HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\System\EnableCdp with enabledValue 1 and disabledValue 0. The app writes EnableCdp = 0 as part of the broader cross-device bundle; the user-side CDP preset values remain tracked only in the deprecated parent audit trail.

allowed_values.label: Not configured
allowed_values.meaning: The device follows the Windows edition default for continue experiences.

allowed_values.label: Continue experiences blocked
allowed_values.meaning: The device is not discoverable by other devices and cannot participate in cross-device experiences.

allowed_values.label: Continue experiences allowed
allowed_values.meaning: The device can participate in cross-device experiences.

app_current_implementation.notes: The app writes EnableCdp = 0 as part of the broader cross-device bundle. This child record documents only the official machine-policy portion of that behavior.

windows_defaults.label: Windows mixed-scope baseline
windows_defaults.applies_to: Windows 10 and newer systems exposing Continue Experiences policy

states.rationale: Microsoft documents the device-policy default as depending on the Windows edition.

windows_defaults.notes: The user-side CDP values remain outside this child record.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows default
intended_for: Systems where cross-device requirements are unknown
avoid_for: Environments that want a strict policy block

states.rationale: Leave the official machine policy unset.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want the documented Windows edition default.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You need a clear and fully sourced preset for cross-device behavior.
tradeoffs: Windows keeps its ordinary policy baseline.
The user-side preset values remain unresolved in the parent audit trail.

recommended_profiles.label: Block cross-device experiences
intended_for: Users who want continue experiences turned off
avoid_for: Anyone expecting the broader preset semantics to be fully official today
recommended_profiles.good_when: You want the documented machine-policy behavior only.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want the legacy mixed preset model that also includes undocumented per-user values.
tradeoffs: The official machine-policy part is clear.
The user-side preset values are intentionally excluded from this child record.

evidence.title: Microsoft ADMX_GroupPolicy Policy CSP: EnableCDP
evidence.summary: Official documentation for the machine policy that controls whether the device can participate in continue experiences.

evidence.title: Local Microsoft GroupPolicy.admx EnableCDP mapping
evidence.summary: The local ADMX file maps EnableCdp to Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\System\EnableCdp with enabledValue 1 and disabledValue 0.

evidence.title: Local Microsoft GroupPolicy.adml EnableCDP help text
evidence.summary: The local ADML help text explains that enabling the policy allows continue experiences, disabling it blocks them, and the not-configured default depends on the Windows edition.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes EnableCdp = 0 under HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\System as part of the broader cross-device bundle.

evidence.title: nohuto win-config mirror - EnableCdp policy
evidence.summary: GroupPolicy.admx shows EnableCdp under HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\System. The mirrored ExplainText says enabling the policy allows the device to participate in cross-device experiences and be discoverable by other Windows devices, while disabling it prevents discoverability and cross-device participation. EnabledValue=1 and DisabledValue=0 are explicit.

validation_proof.notes: Local GroupPolicy.admx also maps EnableCdp with enabledValue 1 and disabledValue 0. The deprecated parent audit trail keeps the mixed user-side CDP preset values separate from this child record. Added nohuto mirror corroboration via nohuto-enablecdp-admx.

decision.why: The official machine policy is clear, documented, and machine-checkable. The unresolved user-side CDP preset values remain in the deprecated parent audit trail rather than this child record.

open_questions: Are the user-side CDP values fully official somewhere, or should they remain documented only as the deprecated parent audit trail?
